prs.title: Standardize Test File Architecture for application Module
prs.body:
## Description

This PR initiates the effort to standardize the test file architecture across the repository by adopting a "Near-Source" testing pattern. The existing test logic for the `application` module has been relocated from a flat `tests.py` file into a dedicated `tests/` directory.

**Key Changes:**
* Relocated `esp/esp/application/tests.py` to `esp/esp/application/tests/test_models.py` to establish a 1:1 mapping with `models.py`.
* Added an `__init__.py` file to the new directory to ensure proper Python package recognition and test discovery.
* Standardized naming to the `test_*.py` format to align with repository-wide testing patterns.

## Related Issue

Closes #5786


## Type of Change

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] New feature
- [ ] Breaking change
- [ ] Documentation update
- [x] Refactor / cleanup
- [ ] CI/CD or build change

## Testing

- [x] Existing tests pass
- [ ] New tests added (if applicable)
- [x] Manually verified

## AI Disclosure

## Checklist

- [x] Self-reviewed the code
- [ ] Updated documentation (if needed)
- [x] No new warnings or errors introduced

prs.title: statistics.py test coverage
prs.body:
## Description

I added unit tests to many uncovered lines from codecov.report  in methods of  `statistics.py` to improve code coverage. 
The new tests specifically check:
* **Zip Code filtering:** Ensuring student locations are counted correctly.
* **Birth Year logic:** Testing how the system counts student ages.
* **Graduation Year logic:** Verifying the graduation data summary.

### the functions include covering tests for missing lines from code.cov report
zipcodes(), demographics(), schools(), startreg(), repeats(), heardabout(), 

I used `SimpleNamespace` to create "dummy" student objects. This allows the tests to run through the complex logic without needing a real database.

## Related Issue

Closes #5329 

## Type of Change

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] New feature
- [ ] Breaking change
- [ ] Documentation update
- [x] Refactor / cleanup (Added Tests)
- [ ] CI/CD or build change

## Testing

- [x] Existing tests pass
- [x] New tests added and tested
- [x] Manually verified (Ran `pytest --cov` to confirm lines are now green)

## AI Disclosure

I used Gemini to understand the statistics.py fore more depth

## Checklist

- [x] Self-reviewed the code
- [x] No new warnings or errors introduced

prs.title: [GSoC] Extends the Structure2d ability to solve for full horizontal analysis of 2d structures
prs.body:
<!-- Your title above should be a short description of what 
was changed. Do not include the issue number in the title. -->

#### References to other Issues or PRs
Fixes #27080  

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed

Integrated the `Column` module into `Structure2D` to enable complete horizontal analysis of 2D structures.  
The `solve_for_reaction_loads` method has been rewritten for robustness, made more user-friendly, and no longer requires users to pass symbols explicitly.  

The `apply_load` method has been enhanced to:  
- Support horizontal load projections via the `Column` class.  
- Allow application of hinges directly to structures.  

The `apply_rotation_hinge` method has been added to:  
- Allow application of hinges directly to structures.  

New analysis methods have been added:  
- `axial_force()`  
- `extension()`  
- `deflection()`  

New plotting methods have been introduced:  
- `plot_extension()`  
- `plot_deflection()`  
- `plot_axial_force()`  
- `plot_bending()`  
- `plot_shear_on_structure()`  
- `plot_axial_force_on_structure()`  
- `plot_bending_on_structure()`  
- `plot_deformation_on_structure()` — visualizes deformation of the structure.  

The `summary` method has been updated to include:  
- Axial force values at points of interest.  
- Bending moment values at middle points, capturing key behavior.  
- Along with the unwrapped 1d position now it shows the 2d position as well

Comprehensive test cases covering all support types and load types have been added for robustness tests including reaction loads, shear_force,axial_force,bending moment..  

#### Other comments
This builds upon #27130 and #28305.  
Detailed changes are highlighted in: [https://github.com/saiudayagiri/sympy/pull/7](https://github.com/saiudayagiri/sympy/pull/7)  

#### Release Notes

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
- physics.continuum_mechanics  
  - Added full horizontal analysis capability to `Structure2D` through integration with the `Column` module.  
  - Rewrote `solve_for_reaction_loads` for improved robustness and ease of use.  
  - Extended `apply_load` to handle horizontal load projections and hinge applications.  
  - Introduced new methods for axial force, extension, and deflection calculations.  
  - Added multiple new plotting utilities, including bending moment plots on structure geometry.  
  - Enhanced `summary` output to include axial force at points of interest and bending moments at middle points and also updated method to support representing 2d co ordinates along with 1d.  
  - Added comprehensive test cases for all support and load types.  
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

prs.title: [GSoC] Updated Column to support application of manual supports using boundary conditions
prs.body:
#### References to other Issues
Fixes #28306 

#### What is fixed or changed
Updated the module to support **manual support application** in addition to the existing `apply_support()` workflow.

Previously, supports were primarily added using `apply_support()`, which automatically generated reaction symbols and applied all necessary boundary conditions internally.  
This PR extends the functionality so that users can now also **manually add supports** by:
- Adding a reaction symbol as a load via `apply_load()`
- Manually specifying the corresponding boundary conditions

**Changes Made:**
- Enhanced support-handling logic to recognize and process manually applied supports
- Updated docstrings to describe both methods (`apply_support()` and manual method)
- Added internal checks to ensure manually defined supports work seamlessly with reaction solving
- Refactored related methods to handle both workflows consistently

**Test Cases Added:**
- Added tests for manual support application workflow

#### Other comments
This builds upon #28304 and the changes are highlighted here saiudayagiri#5

#### Release Notes
<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* physics.continuum_mechanics

  * Added support for manual definition of supports using `apply_load()` and explicit boundary conditions, alongside the existing `apply_support()` method.
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

prs.title: [GSoC] changes method name from deflection to extension in column module as it is more appropriate for axial displacement
prs.body:
#### References to other Issues  
Fixes #28303

#### What is fixed or changed
Renamed the `deflection()` method in the `Column` module to `extension()` to better reflect its functionality of calculating **axial displacement**.  
The previous name was misleading since:  
- The method deals with axial displacement, not vertical deflection.  
- A method with the same name, `deflection()`, exists in the `Beam` module, where it correctly refers to vertical deflection.

**Changes Made:**
- Renamed `Column.deflection()` → `Column.extension()`
- Updated method docstrings and boundary condition references to use the term “extension” instead of “deflection”

**Test Cases Added:**
- updated tests to ensure the renamed method works as expected  
- Verified no functional changes beyond method naming


#### Release Notes
<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* physics.continuum_mechanics

  * Renamed `deflection()` in the `Column` module to `extension()` to clearly indicate it calculates axial displacement.
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

prs.title: [GSoC] Updated solve_for_reaction_loads() method to avoid confusion to users for passing symbols
prs.body:
#### References to other Issues 
Fixes #26604

#### what is fixed or changed
Updated solve_for_reaction_loads() method to use apply_support() reaction symbols to track internally how it does with rotation_hinge_symbols and sliding_hinge_symbols and still allows users to pass supports as loads like before 
**Changes Made:**

- Added applied_support_symbols to track all generated reaction symbols internally
- modified solve_for_reaction_loads internally passed applied_support_symbols along with reactions
- updated few other methods to support this
- Updated both docs and .rst file 


**Test Cases Added:**


#### Other comments
No functional impact outside of clearer error reporting.

#### Release Notes
<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* physics.continuum_mechanics

  * Updated the module to make it user friendly and to avoid passing support symbols to solve_for_reaction_loads()
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

prs.title: [GSoC] Updated max_bmoment() function to handle zero constant regions
prs.body:
## Enhanced the `max_bmoment method

Fixes  #28211  

### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
The previous `max_bmoment` implementation failed to return desired result when there are zero constant regions it instead ran infinite loops.

### The new version
- Skips flat-zero regions 
- finds all points and intervals where maximum bending moment occurs and returns the result to give user to easily study his/her beam systems behaviour.

### Other comments
None.

### Release Notes
<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* physics.continuum_mechanics

  * Updated max_bmoment() that can now handle zero or constant over regions and can return all points and intervals where maximum bending moment occurs.
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

prs.title: [GSOC] Changed the Symbol setting of Length as Positive as optional choice
prs.body:
#### References to other Issues or PRs
Fixes #24042

#### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
This pull request updates **beam.py** to automatically assume a positive beam length for symbolic computations, preventing infinite loops during limit evaluations of `SingularityFunction`. Specifically:

- Introduced a `_wrap_positive` helper in `Beam.__init__` to wrap any non-positive length symbol with an internally positive-assumed counterpart.
- Mapped user-provided length symbols to these internal positive symbols and restored the original symbols in all returned results.
- Added tests for `solve_for_reaction_loads` verifying correct reaction calculations with symbolic lengths, both with and without explicit `positive=True`.


#### Other comments
No backward-incompatible API changes were introduced. Existing numeric uses remain unaffected.

#### Release Notes

<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
NO ENTRY
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

prs.title: [GSoC]Changed the point_cflexure method
prs.body:
## Enhanced the `point_cflexure` method

Fixes  #26634

### Brief description of what is fixed or changed
The previous `point_cflexure` implementation simply returned all algebraic roots of the bending-moment expression and did not verify that the moment actually changes sign at those points. It also failed (raising `NotImplementedError`) when an entire span of the beam or a region  had M(x)=0

### The new version
- Uses `solveset` on each piecewise segment to find isolated roots or intervals.  
- Skips flat-zero regions before solving.

### Other comments
None.

### Release Notes
<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->
* physics.continuum_mechanics

  * Fixed incorrect point_cflexure() that can now handle zero or constant over regions and strictly checks the sign change across the points
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

prs.title: Perf: Enhanced the draw function in the beam module with Matplotlib for imp…
prs.body:
#Perf: Improve draw Method in Beam Module with Matplotlib

## References to Other Issues or PRs
None, just improving the `draw` method.

## Brief Description of What is Fixed or Changed
This PR enhances the `draw` method in `sympy/physics/continuum_mechanics/beam.py` using **Matplotlib**, making beam diagrams clearer, better structured, and more visually intuitive.

### **Key Improvements**
- **Beam Representation**:  
  - Changed from a rectangle to a thick line.  
  - Uses alternating colors to highlight **joints and hinges**.  
- **Loads and Reactions**:  
  - **Point Loads**: Fixed-size arrows (blue/black for loads, gray for reactions) for better readability.  
  - **Moments**: Now shown as curved arrows (counterclockwise for positive, clockwise for negative) instead of circular markers.  
  - **Distributed Loads**: Displayed as shaded areas with arrows (red/black), supporting ramp loads.  
  - **Load Heights**: Previously dynamic based on magnitude, causing small loads (e.g., **10 compared to 100**) to merge into the beam.  
    - **Now all loads have a uniform height**, as their numerical values are shown anyway.  
- **Support Representation**:  
  - **Pin Support** → Square .  
  - **Fixed Support** → Square.  
  - **Roller Support** → Triangle.  
- **Scaling and Visibility**:  
  - Automatic scaling based on beam length and load sizes, with extra padding.  
  - Moments positioned below the axis for better visibility.  

### **New Features**
- **Customization Options**:  
  - Toggle visibility for **supports, reactions, load values, and grid lines**.  
  - Show load magnitudes as text next to arrows.  
  - Symbolic loads now use **black arrows** for easy recognition.  

### **Key Fixes**
- **Better Load Representation**: Previously, load heights **varied dynamically**, causing small loads to become nearly invisible.  
  - Now, all loads appear with **equal arrow height**, making diagrams clearer without distorting relative magnitudes.  

These changes make beam diagrams **easier to read, visually intuitive, and user-friendly** while maintaining compatibility with the `Beam` class.  


## Release Notes
## Release Notes  
<!-- BEGIN RELEASE NOTES -->  
* physics.continuum_mechanics  
  * Improved the `draw` method in the `Beam` class using **Matplotlib**, enhancing clarity and adding customization options.  
  * Loads now appear with a **uniform height**, preventing small loads from merging into the beam.  
  * Supports, reactions, and load values can now be toggled for **better user control**.  
  * Enhanced visual representation of supports, moments, and distributed loads.  
<!-- END RELEASE NOTES -->

issues.title: [Improvement]: Standardize Test File Architecture across the Repository
issues.body:
### Problem Statement

### Description
The test file organization across the repository currently follows inconsistent naming and placement conventions. In several modules, there is no clear mapping between source files and their corresponding tests, which makes it difficult to track coverage and maintain the codebase.

I propose standardizing the test hierarchy by adopting a "Near-Source" testing pattern.

### Proposed Standard
Each major source directory should contain a dedicated `tests/` subdirectory with test files mapped 1:1 to the source files they cover.

**Example Structure:**
```text
folder/
├── module_a.py
├── module_b.py
└── tests/
    ├── test_module_a.py
    └── test_module_b.py
```

### Proposed Solution

### Proposed Solution
To resolve the current inconsistencies, we should implement a uniform directory structure for testing across all modules. The solution involves:

* **Creating Dedicated Test Directories**: Every major directory containing source code should have a nested `tests/` folder.
* **Standardizing File Naming**: Test files should mirror their source file counterparts with a `test_` prefix (e.g., `views.py` becomes `tests/test_views.py`).
* **Relocating Existing Tests**: Move scattered `tests.py` files or incorrectly placed test scripts into the new standardized hierarchy to ensure consistency.
* **Enforcing 1:1 Mapping**: Ensure that every new feature or module introduced has a corresponding test file in the local `tests/` directory.
* **Refactoring High-Impact Modules**: Start the implementation with the `program` and `customforms` modules to establish the pattern in the most critical areas of the codebase.

### Alternatives Considered

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_

issues.title: [Task] Increase Unit Test Coverage for statistics.py (Demographics & Zipcodes)
issues.body:
### Problem Statement

## Problem
The `statistics.py` module currently has several logic paths that are not covered by unit tests from the codecov report.

## Proposed Changes
Add unit tests to `esp/program/tests/test_statistics.py` 

## Impact
- Increases code coverage for the `program` module.
- Prevents regressions in the demographics reporting dashboard.
- Uses `SimpleNamespace` for lightweight mocking to keep tests fast.


### Proposed Solution

- Add unit tests to `esp/program/tests/test_statistics.py` 
- Increases code coverage for the `program` module.

### Alternatives Considered

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_

issues.title: misleading error for solve_for_reaction_loads in beam module for inconsistent systems
issues.body:
when the system is inconsistent or symbolic equations contradict each other the solve_for_reaction_loads fails and leads to key index errors which is confusing and not user friendly
The below problems shows the result

**Problem 1 is a symbolic problem**
```

E, I, P = Symbol('E'), Symbol('I'), Symbol('P')
b = Beam(10, E, I)
r1 = b.apply_support(0, 'pin')
r2 = b.apply_support(10, 'roller')
b.apply_rotation_hinge(4)
b.apply_load(-P, 4, -1)

b.solve_for_reaction_loads(r1, r2)
print(b.reaction_loads)
```
Output
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
IndexError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[2], line 9
      7 b.apply_load(-P, 4, -1)
 
----> 9 b.solve_for_reaction_loads(r1, r2)
     10 print(b.reaction_loads)

File c:\Users\saiba\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python313\Lib\site-packages\sympy\physics\continuum_mechanics\beam.py:977, in Beam.solve_for_reaction_loads(self, *reactions)
    974     eqs = deflection_curve.subs(x, position) - value
    975     deflection_eqs.append(eqs)
--> 977 solution = list((linsolve([shear_curve, moment_curve] + shear_force_eqs + bending_moment_eqs + slope_eqs
    978                     + deflection_eqs, (C3, C4) + reactions + rotation_jumps + deflection_jumps).args)[0])
    979 reaction_index = 2+len(reactions)
    980 rotation_index = reaction_index + len(rotation_jumps)

IndexError: tuple index out of range

**Problem2 is numerical problem**
```
E, I = Symbol('E'), Symbol('I')
b = Beam(10, E, I)
R1=b.apply_support(0,'pin')
b.apply_load(10, 10, -1)
b.solve_for_reaction_loads(R1)
```
output
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
IndexError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[4], line 7
      5 b.apply_load(10, 10, -1)
----> 7 b.solve_for_reaction_loads(R1)

File c:\Users\saiba\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python313\Lib\site-packages\sympy\physics\continuum_mechanics\beam.py:977, in Beam.solve_for_reaction_loads(self, *reactions)
    974     eqs = deflection_curve.subs(x, position) - value
    975     deflection_eqs.append(eqs)
--> 977 solution = list((linsolve([shear_curve, moment_curve] + shear_force_eqs + bending_moment_eqs + slope_eqs
    978                     + deflection_eqs, (C3, C4) + reactions + rotation_jumps + deflection_jumps).args)[0])
    979 reaction_index = 2+len(reactions)
    980 rotation_index = reaction_index + len(rotation_jumps)

IndexError: tuple index out of range

From a bit of research it is understood that the linsolve returns no sultion for inconsistent systems and accesing solution from empty list is leading to key index error 

solution = list((linsolve([shear_curve, moment_curve] + shear_force_eqs + bending_moment_eqs + slope_eqs
                   + deflection_eqs, (C3, C4) + reactions + rotation_jumps + deflection_jumps).args)[0])

issues.title: The Column Module does not support manual support application like Beam module supports
issues.body:
**Issue:**
The `Column` module currently lacks support for **manual support application**, unlike the `Beam` module, which creates the following problems:

1. **Missing feature** – Manual support application (adding reaction symbols via `apply_load()` and manually defining boundary conditions) is not supported in `Column`.
2. **Inconsistency between modules** – The `Beam` module supports both `apply_support()` and manual support methods, while `Column` supports only `apply_support()`. This inconsistency can cause confusion for users expecting similar workflows.
3. **Integration issues in `Structure2D`** – The `Structure2D` module integrates `Beam` and `Column` functionality. It uses the existing manual support application from `Beam` for its beam components, but since `Column` lacks this feature, it leads to inconsistent behavior and issues when integrating column supports.

issues.title: [GSoC] Confusing  method name in Column module deflection for axial displacement instead of extension or displacement
issues.body:
**Issue:**  
In the current `Column` module, there is a method that calculates **axial displacement** for a column. However:  
- The method name and its boundary conditions are labeled as `deflection`, which is misleading.  
- A similarly named method, `deflection()`, also exists in the `Beam` module, where it correctly refers to **vertical deflection**.  

**Suggestion:**  
Rename the method in the `Column` module from `deflection()` to `extension()` to:  
- Accurately reflect its purpose (axial displacement).  
- Avoid confusion with the `Beam` module’s vertical `deflection()` method.

issues.body:
**Issue in max_bending_moment()
when there is a constant zero region involved in the Bending_moment It is looping infinite and not returning the answer
```
from sympy import symbols, Symbol
from sympy.physics.continuum_mechanics.beam import Beam
E = Symbol('E')
I = Symbol('I')
b = Beam(15, E, I)
r0 = b.apply_support(0, type='pin')
r10 = b.apply_support(10, type='pin')
r15, m15 = b.apply_support(15, type='fixed')
b.apply_rotation_hinge(5)
b.apply_rotation_hinge(12)
b.apply_load(-10, 5, -1)
b.apply_load(-5, 10, 0, 15)
b.solve_for_reaction_loads(r0, r10, r15, m15)
b.plot_bending_moment()
print(b.max_bending_moment())
```

issues.title: Integrate Column Module into Structure2D to Enable Full Horizontal Reaction Analysis
issues.body:
The current Structure2D PR #27130  uses the Beam module—correctly computing vertical reactions and bending moments. However, for horizontal reactions, it uses only a simplified Newton’s law approach and solves for just one horizontal reaction, regardless of the number of support points. This restricts the analysis to trivial cases and limits its ability to model real-world problems with multiple supports.

Recently the Column module has been implemented and ready for review in a separate PR #28138 .and now this can be integrated with Structure2d for horizontal analysis

issues.title: apply_support() treats invalid input as fixed support and causes confusing KeyIndexError in solve_for_reaction_loads()
issues.body:
When calling Beam.apply_support() with a typo in the support type (e.g., "pen" instead of "pin"), the current implementation silently defaults to a fixed support. Rather than raising a clear user error, it leads to a KeyIndexError originating from solve_for_reaction_loads(), which misleads users and makes debugging difficult.

The same issue occurs in the join() method—incorrect or unsupported values aren’t validated, potentially resulting in unexpected behavior without clear error messages.
